title: Using hardcoded sload(0) or sload(1) for named storage variables
trigger_patterns: contract state variable in assembly block
explanation: Solidity assigns storage slots based on declaration order, inheritance, and packing. Hardcoding slot numbers like sload(0) only works if token is the very first declared variable with no inheritance. Adding a single inherited contract or reordering declarations silently breaks the slot assumptions, causing reads from wrong slots with no compilation error.
why_wrong: Storage layout is determined by the compiler and can change silently when: (1) base contracts are added or reordered, (2) new variables are inserted before existing ones, (3) struct packing changes. Hardcoded slot numbers create invisible time bombs that survive compilation and only fail at runtime.
better_approach: For frequently read addresses that never change after deployment, use 'immutable' — this stores the value in contract bytecode instead of storage, saving one SLOAD (800 gas on Mantle) per read with zero risk of slot collision.
gas_impact: Using immutable saves 800 gas per read on Mantle vs sload. Using .slot syntax has zero gas impact but is safe.
